---
title: "The statistical modelling suite"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
`nitrix.stats` is one family of models, not a pile of unrelated fitters. Almost
every model here is solved by the *same two moves*:

1. **Solve a weighted normal-equations system** $(X^{\top} W X + P)\,\beta = X^{\top} W z$ — a small $p\times p$ linear solve you already understand from least squares.
2. **Choose the extra parameters** (a noise variance, a smoothing strength, a random-effect variance) by maximising a likelihood with a cheap iteration.

The chapters below climb a ladder — GLM → LME → GAM → GLMM → GAMM → HSGP — and
at each rung only *one new idea* is added to those two moves. Read GLM first;
everything else is "GLM plus one thing".
:::

This suite is written for a reader who is **comfortable with linear algebra**
(matrices, least squares, eigendecomposition) but has **no formal training in
statistics or numerical methods**. We build every model up from the linear
algebra you know, and we explain each statistical idea and each solver the first
time it appears.

## The ladder

Every model is a linear predictor $X\beta$ (a matrix times a coefficient
vector) wrapped in successively more structure. Each rung reuses the rung below
and adds exactly one ingredient:

| Rung | Model | The one new ingredient |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GLM** — generalised linear model | a *link* + *non-Gaussian noise* (so we fit by **IRLS**) |
| 2 | **LME** — linear mixed-effects | *random effects* $Z$ (correlated noise → **variance components**, REML) |
| 3 | **GAM** — generalised additive model | *smooth terms* (a wiggliness **penalty** $P$ on the coefficients) |
| 4 | **GLMM** — generalised linear mixed model | GLM **and** LME at once (non-Gaussian + random effects) |
| 5 | **GAMM** — generalised additive mixed model | smooths **and** random effects (they turn out to be the same thing) |
| 6 | **HSGP** — Hilbert-space Gaussian process | a *kernel* prior, written as just another penalty |

The surprising payoff, made precise in the GAM chapter: **a smoothing penalty
and a random-effect variance are the same object seen from two directions.**
That single equivalence is why GAM, LME, GLMM, GAMM and HSGP share one engine
instead of being six separate programs.

## The shared machinery

Because the rungs reuse each other, a handful of algorithms do the heavy lifting
across the whole suite. Each is introduced where it first naturally arises and
reused (by cross-reference) thereafter:

| Tool | What it does | Introduced in |
|---|---|---|
| **IRLS** (iteratively reweighted least squares) | turns a non-Gaussian fit into a sequence of ordinary weighted least-squares solves | [GLM](glm.qmd) |
| **Damped Newton** | makes that iteration converge reliably even when a step would overshoot | [GLM](glm.qmd) |
| **cuSOLVER-free small-linalg** | does every $p\times p$ solve without the vendor GPU solver, so fits run on accelerators where it is broken | [GLM](glm.qmd) |
| **FaST-LMM spectral trick** | diagonalises the random-effect covariance once so each fit is elementwise-cheap | LME |
| **Fellner–Schall** | the multiplicative update that chooses smoothing / variance parameters | [smoothing parameters](fellner-schall.qmd) |
| **Block-Woodbury / Schur** | inverts a huge "many groups" covariance by only touching small blocks | GLMM |

## How `nitrix` runs these at scale

One detail colours every chapter. `nitrix.stats` is **mass-univariate**: the
same design matrix $X$ is fit to *every* element of an image (every voxel,
vertex, or fixel) at once — hundreds of thousands of tiny regressions sharing
one design. Two consequences recur throughout:

- The expensive, shared pieces (a factorisation of $X$, an eigendecomposition of
  the random-effect design) are computed **once** and reused across all
  elements; only the per-element response changes.
- Every fit runs a **fixed number of iterations** so it can be `vmap`-mapped and
  JIT-compiled across the whole image, and every linear solve avoids the vendor
  GPU solver (`cuSOLVER`) so it stays portable across accelerators.

## Reading paths

- **Fast path** — read each chapter's TL;DR, plus the GLM chapter in full. That
  is enough to use the suite and to know which model fits which question.
- **Thorough path** — read in ladder order. Each chapter assumes the one below
  it and points back to the shared-machinery page when an algorithm recurs.

## Chapters

- [Generalised linear models — the foundation](glm.qmd) *(start here)*
- [Choosing smoothing parameters: the Fellner–Schall method](fellner-schall.qmd)
- *Linear mixed-effects models* — coming next
- *Generalised additive models* · *GLMMs* · *GAMMs* · *Hilbert-space GPs* —
  building up the ladder
